confirmed.title: Dead forwarding_number fallback: emergency transfer tool and prompt silently ignore the saved fallback number
confirmed.evidence:
_fetch_kz_config select (lines 377-387): "... emergency_surcharge_text, incoming_forwarding_number, price_info_enabled, conversation_logic, conversation_logic_enabled" — no `forwarding_number`.

build_transfer_tool (line 1321): emergency = _dial_clean(cfg.get("emergency_number") or cfg.get("forwarding_number"))
render_emergency_block (line 794): if (cfg.get("emergency_number") or cfg.get("forwarding_number") or "").strip():

kiki_zentrale.py PATCH /phone docstring (lines 1243-1244): "- `forwarding_number` / `incoming_forwarding_number` → `agent_configs` (these drive the transferCall tool at runtime)."
confirmed.claim: `_fetch_kz_config` (agent_config.py:371-394) never selects the `forwarding_number` column, yet both `build_transfer_tool` (line 1321) and `render_emergency_block` (line 794) use `cfg.get("forwarding_number")` as the fallback emergency target when `emergency_number` is unset. Since the dict can never contain that key, the fallback is dead code: an org that enabled Notdienst and saved only `forwarding_number` via PATCH /api/kiki-zentrale/phone (whose docstring explicitly claims forwarding_number "drives the transferCall tool at runtime") gets NO `transfer_to_number` system tool synced, and the rendered prompt takes the line-803 branch telling the agent "Es ist KEINE Notdienst-Nummer hinterlegt — leite NICHT weiter." Real emergency callers are downgraded to a callback note instead of being bridged, with no error or warning anywhere — the save reports success and sync-status reports applied.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed: _fetch_kz_config (agent_config.py:377-387) omits forwarding_number from its select, so cfg.get("forwarding_number") at lines 794 and 1321 is always None — the legacy fallback is dead. No rescue: with no emergency_number the prompt takes the "KEINE Notdienst-Nummer — leite NICHT weiter" branch and no transfer tool entry is synced, so the legacy webhook in transfer.py (which DOES honor forwarding_number) is never invoked either. Severity downgraded from high to medium because the current Telefon UI only writes incoming_forwarding_number — only legacy orgs that set forwarding_number and never set emergency_number (with emergency_enabled on) are affected, though for them real emergencies silently degrade to a callback note.

confirmed.title: PATCH /leitfaden no_prices guard fires AFTER the row writes: partial save committed, repush skipped, 422 returned
confirmed.claim: In save_leitfaden, the per-row updates (sort_order for every row, is_active for non-linked rows) are executed inside the loop BEFORE the price_info guard runs. When the org admin's batch includes activating the price-info linked row but no priced catalog items exist, `_do` returns "no_prices" AFTER all row updates are already committed (no transaction/rollback). The handler then raises 422 — telling the user the save failed — and skips `_schedule_repush`, so the Leitfaden order/active toggles that DID land in agent_required_fields are never re-rendered into the ElevenLabs prompt. Result: DB and live agent diverge silently (the prompt still reflects the old field order until some unrelated save triggers the next repush), and the accumulated cfg_updates for other linked rows (appointments_enabled, kva_enabled) in the same batch are dropped while their row positions were saved — an inconsistent half-applied state presented to the user as a clean failure.

verdict.reasoning: Verified in kiki_zentrale.py lines 639-689: per-row updates execute as individual autocommitted PostgREST calls inside the loop before the no_prices guard; on "no_prices" the handler raises 422 and skips both _upsert_config (dropping appointments_enabled/kva_enabled toggles batched in cfg_updates) and _schedule_repush, so saved sort_order changes never reach the agent prompt while the user is told the save failed. No transaction, rollback, or upstream guard prevents this. Downgraded to low because linked-row active states stay consistent (GET mirrors agent_configs, which was never changed) and any subsequent successful save repushes the full config, self-healing the prompt divergence.

confirmed.title: PATCH /verhalten: DB write commits, then an ElevenLabs patch failure aborts the handler before the sync/repush is scheduled — silent DB↔agent divergence with no banner
confirmed.evidence:
def _do() -> dict:
    cfg = _upsert_config(user.org_id, supa) if supa else None
    agent_state = None
    if el_patch:
        agent_id = ea.get_org_agent_id(user.org_id)
        ea.patch_agent_safely(... endpoint_label="verhalten")
        ...
result = await run_in_threadpool(_do)
# The per-capability autonomy (and welcome) feed the agent prompt → re-render
if supa:
    _schedule_repush(background, user, "kz_verhalten")
confirmed.claim: update_verhalten writes the Supabase config first (`_upsert_config(user.org_id, supa)`), then performs the direct ElevenLabs patch for persona_name/first_message/language/voice_id inside the same `_do`. If that EL call raises (EL outage, get_org_agent_id failure for an un-provisioned org, verify failure), the exception propagates out of `run_in_threadpool` and the handler returns 500 — but the autonomy/welcome DB write is already committed, and the lines `if supa: _schedule_repush(...)` are never reached. So prompt-feeding fields (appointments_level, kva_level, welcome_message, reschedule_request_timeout_hours, …) change in the DB while the live agent prompt is never re-rendered, AND no agent_sync 'pending'/'failed' state is ever set (begin_sync is only called inside _schedule_repush). The frontend sync banner stays idle, so neither the user nor the retry button knows the agent is now running stale behaviour — the exact divergence the sync-status machinery was built to surface. Every other mutating handler in this file orders DB-write → schedule_repush with no intervening fallible EL call; only /verhalten has this gap.

verdict.reasoning: Traced update_verhalten (kiki_zentrale.py ~460-512): inside _do, _upsert_config commits the supa fields (welcome_message/welcome_messages/autonomy levels) to agent_configs first; then patch_agent_safely can raise (CrossOrgAgentWriteError, EL HTTP/verify failures, get_org_agent_id for un-provisioned org) — none caught — so the exception escapes run_in_threadpool and `if supa: _schedule_repush(...)` at line 511 is never reached; begin_sync is only called inside _schedule_repush, so no pending/failed sync state is ever recorded. The mixed payload is the normal case: VerhaltenSection.tsx always includes welcome_message/welcome_messages and adds persona/voice/language only when changed, so supa+el_patch travel together. Only caveat softening the claim: the user does see a 'Speichern fehlgeschlagen' toast from the 500, so it is not fully silent at save time — but the committed DB change with no re-render and an idle sync banner (the divergence the sync machinery exists to surface) is real until some later save triggers a repush.

confirmed.title: agent_sync_seq guard serializes only the status row, not the ElevenLabs writes — a slow stale push can overwrite a newer prompt and still show 'applied'
confirmed.evidence:
def finish_sync(org_id, seq, *, ok, reason=None):
    """Resolve a pending sync — guarded by seq (stale completions no-op)."""
    ...
    db.table("agent_configs").update({"agent_sync_status": "applied" if ok else "failed", ...})
    .eq("org_id", str(org_id)).eq("agent_sync_seq", seq).execute()

# rerender_and_push_for_org (1578-1598): renders prompt_text from DB, then
# patch_agent_safely(... {"prompt": prompt_text} ...) — no seq/version check
# before or during the EL PATCH.
confirmed.claim: Two overlapping saves spawn two independent background `_repush_bg` tasks (kiki_zentrale.py:71-113). Each task independently re-renders the prompt from the DB and PATCHes ElevenLabs; the only ordering protection is `finish_sync`'s `.eq("agent_sync_seq", seq)` on the status row. Concretely: save A (seq 5) starts its bg task and reads config state X; save B then commits config state Y, begins seq 6, and its bg task renders and PATCHes Y and resolves status='applied'. If A's task was slower (EL retry, snapshot GET stall, threadpool scheduling), A's PATCH carrying the OLD render X lands on the agent AFTER B's — patch_agent_safely verifies only against its own write, so it succeeds. End state: ElevenLabs runs the stale prompt X, the DB holds config Y, sync-status reads 'applied', and A's `finish_sync(seq=5)` silently no-ops. The seq guard makes the BANNER last-write-wins but does not make the AGENT last-write-wins; there is no config-version stamp in the pushed prompt and no per-org lock around the render→PATCH window, so a rapid double-save can leave the live agent permanently divergent while reporting success.

verdict.reasoning: Traced kiki_zentrale.py _schedule_repush → _repush_bg → rerender_and_push_for_org → patch_agent_safely: there is no per-org lock, no version stamp in the pushed prompt, and patch_agent_safely verifies only against its own GET/PATCH pair, so two concurrent background tasks can interleave and a slow stale push (or its verify-rollback via _restore_full) can leave the EL agent on an old prompt while finish_sync's seq guard makes only the status banner last-write-wins, reporting 'applied'. Minor overstatement: the stale window starts at the bg task's DB read (it re-renders fresh at execution), not at save time, but the window spans multiple EL round-trips so the race is reachable. Self-heals on the next save and requires a rapid double-save by the same org admin, so medium severity stands.

confirmed.title: begin_sync runs up to 3 synchronous Supabase round-trips directly on the event loop in every mutating Kiki-Zentrale handler
confirmed.evidence:
def _schedule_repush(background, user, endpoint_label) -> None:
    """begin_sync (synchronous, so the first poll already sees 'pending') + ..."""
    seq = ac.begin_sync(user.org_id, endpoint_label)
    background.add_task(_repush_bg, user.org_id, user.id, endpoint_label, seq)

# called as plain sync code from async handlers, e.g. update_verhalten:
#     result = await run_in_threadpool(_do)
#     if supa:
#         _schedule_repush(background, user, "kz_verhalten")

# ac.begin_sync (agent_config.py:1480-1504) issues: a .select() on agent_configs,
# a conditional .upsert(), and a .rpc("kz_begin_agent_sync") — all via the
# synchronous supabase client.
confirmed.claim: `_schedule_repush` is invoked directly (not via run_in_threadpool) from every async config-mutating handler (update_verhalten:511, save_leitfaden:688, update_conversation_logic:851, update_emergency:1232, update_phone:1286, retry_sync:421, etc.). It synchronously calls `ac.begin_sync`, which performs up to three blocking Supabase HTTP round-trips (select, conditional upsert, rpc) on the asyncio event loop. This is the exact anti-pattern this codebase already fixed elsewhere (the sync supabase client must only run via run_in_threadpool, otherwise every concurrent request on the worker stalls for the duration of the DB calls). The careful run_in_threadpool wrapping of `_do` in each handler is undone two lines later. Under load — or a slow Supabase round-trip — all in-flight requests on the worker freeze for the begin_sync duration; the deliberate design note 'synchronous, so the first poll already sees pending' could be preserved with `await run_in_threadpool(ac.begin_sync, ...)`.

verdict.reasoning: Verified end-to-end: handlers like update_verhalten (kiki_zentrale.py:511) and retry_sync (:421) are `async def` and call `_schedule_repush` (:107-113) inline after `await run_in_threadpool(_do)`. `_schedule_repush` synchronously calls `ac.begin_sync` (agent_config.py:1480-1504), which uses `get_service_client()` — the synchronous Supabase client — for a select, a conditional upsert, and an rpc, all blocking HTTP round-trips executed directly on the event loop. No upstream guard offloads this; the project's own convention (sync client only via run_in_threadpool) is violated here. Impact is worker-wide latency under slow Supabase, not data corruption or security, so "low" severity is correct.

confirmed.title: Conversation reload silently drops the NEWEST messages once a chat exceeds 200 rows
confirmed.evidence:
messages = (
    client.table("copilot_messages")
    .select("id, role, content, tool_calls, created_at")
    .eq("conversation_id", conversation_id)
    .order("created_at").limit(200).execute().data or []
)
confirmed.claim: GET /api/copilot/conversations/{id} orders messages by created_at ASCENDING and applies limit(200) with no pagination. For a conversation with more than 200 messages (100 turns — each /chat turn inserts 2 rows), the limit keeps the OLDEST 200 rows and silently discards the most recent ones. The frontend (CopilotPanel.openConversation, frontend/src/components/copilot/CopilotPanel.tsx:152-179) renders exactly what it receives with no truncation indicator, so the user reopens a long chat and sees only its old beginning while the recent exchange has vanished; worse, send() then rebuilds the model history from these displayed messages (CopilotPanel.tsx:239-241), so the AI also loses the newest context. If truncation must happen it should be `desc=True` + reverse (keep newest), or paginate.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the query at copilot.py:143-148: ascending order + limit(200) keeps the OLDEST 200 rows; _persist_turn inserts 2 rows per turn with no cap or pruning, so conversations can exceed 200. CopilotPanel.openConversation renders exactly what it receives with no truncation indicator, and send() rebuilds model history from those messages, so reopening a long chat silently loses the newest messages and AI context. No upstream guard or pagination refutes the claim; medium severity is appropriate (silent data-display loss, no security impact).

confirmed.title: User and assistant rows of one turn share an identical created_at, making reload ordering within a turn nondeterministic
confirmed.evidence:
client.table("copilot_messages").insert([
    {"conversation_id": cid, "org_id": user.org_id, "role": "user", "content": message},
    {
        "conversation_id": cid, "org_id": user.org_id, "role": "assistant", ...
]).execute()
-- 0042: created_at timestamptz not null default now()
-- read path: .order("created_at").limit(200)
confirmed.claim: Both rows of a turn are inserted in a single batch INSERT, so both get created_at from default now(), which is the transaction start time — identical to the microsecond for the two rows. The read path in get_conversation orders by created_at only, with no secondary tiebreaker (no id, no role ordering), and Postgres sorting is not stable, so a reloaded conversation can render the assistant answer BEFORE the user question within a turn. Since the frontend rebuilds the model history from the reloaded order (CopilotPanel.tsx:239-241), a flipped pair also feeds the model an inverted Q/A sequence on continued chats. Fix is a deterministic tiebreaker (e.g. order by created_at, then a per-batch sequence or role) or per-row timestamps.

verdict.reasoning: Verified in 0042_ai_copilot.sql: copilot_messages.created_at defaults to now() (transaction-start, identical for both rows of the batch insert at copilot.py:87) and the PK is a random uuid. get_conversation (copilot.py:147) orders by created_at only, with no tiebreaker, and the frontend does not re-sort. Postgres gives unspecified order for equal keys, so user/assistant rows of a turn can flip on reload. Real but cosmetic/low-impact, so low severity stands.

confirmed.title: GET/DELETE /api/copilot/conversations/{id} return 500 instead of 404 for any non-UUID id
confirmed.evidence:
async def get_conversation(conversation_id: str, user: CurrentUser = Depends(require_org)) -> dict:
    def _do() -> dict | None:
        convo = (
            client.table("copilot_conversations").select("id, title")
            .eq("id", conversation_id).eq("org_id", user.org_id)...
confirmed.claim: The conversation_id path parameter is typed plain `str` (not uuid.UUID) and is passed directly into a PostgREST `.eq("id", ...)` filter against a uuid column. For any value that is not a valid UUID (e.g. GET /api/copilot/conversations/abc), PostgREST rejects the request with 'invalid input syntax for type uuid' and supabase-py raises postgrest APIError inside _do; neither get_conversation nor delete_conversation catches it and app/main.py registers no global APIError exception handler, so the endpoint returns a 500 with a traceback in the logs instead of the intended 404/422. Contrast with the chat path, where _persist_turn wraps the same pattern in try/except. Declaring the param as uuid.UUID (FastAPI returns 422) or catching APIError fixes it.

verdict.reasoning: copilot.py:133/161 declare conversation_id as plain str and pass it straight into .eq("id", ...) on copilot_conversations.id, which migration 0062 defines as uuid. PostgREST returns 22P02 "invalid input syntax for type uuid" for non-UUID values; supabase-py raises APIError from .execute(), and neither _do nor any global handler catches it (app/main.py registers no exception_handler/APIError handler), so FastAPI returns 500 instead of 404/422. Org scoping is intact, so impact is limited to an unhandled-error/log-noise bug — low severity as claimed.

confirmed.claim: The whole persistence sequence (ownership check, conversation insert, messages insert, updated_at touch) runs as three non-transactional PostgREST calls under one blanket except that returns the ORIGINAL `conversation_id` parameter, not the freshly created `cid`. If the messages insert or the updated_at update fails after a new conversation row was created (transient DB/network error), (a) an empty conversation row is permanently orphaned — it appears in the history list and opens as a blank thread (CopilotPanel renders zero messages, not even the greeting), and (b) the client receives conversation_id=None back from /chat, so the next turn creates yet another new conversation, fragmenting one user session across multiple rows. Nothing is logged (bare `except Exception: return`), so these failures are invisible in ops.

verdict.reasoning: The trace holds: in `_persist_turn` (copilot.py:66-105) the conversation insert, messages insert, and updated_at touch are three separate PostgREST calls under one blanket `except Exception: return conversation_id`. If the messages insert fails after the `copilot_conversations` insert succeeded, the freshly created `cid` is discarded and the original (typically None) is returned to /chat (line 114-115), so the client starts a new conversation next turn — and the empty orphan row IS surfaced by `list_conversations` (lines 121-127 select all conversations with no message-count filter) and opens via `get_conversation` as a thread with zero messages. No upstream guard, transaction, or DB constraint prevents this, and the bare except logs nothing. It only manifests on transient mid-sequence failures and chat itself is unaffected (deliberately best-effort), so severity low is correct.

confirmed.title: Confirmed copilot writes lose their conversation link and the audit insert is fail-open with no logging

confirmed.title: Conversation list is hard-capped at 30 with no pagination — older chats become invisible and undeletable while rows grow unbounded
confirmed.evidence:

    .select("id, title, created_at, updated_at")
    .eq("org_id", user.org_id).eq("user_id", user.id)

Every 'Neuer Chat' + first message creates a new copilot_conversations row (plus 2 message rows per turn) with no retention policy, cleanup job, or per-user cap anywhere in the codebase (no other code path deletes these tables besides the single-id DELETE endpoint). confirmed.title: Live-fill payload is never consumed when the form route is already mounted — fallback API write plus a stale sessionStorage payload that auto-creates a duplicate invoice/KVA on the next visit, with no confirmation
confirmed.evidence:

    appendStep(msgId, `→ Formular geöffnet: ${route}`)
    navigate(route)
    ...
    const timeout = setTimeout(() => {
      if (settled) return
      settled = true
      window.removeEventListener(LIVE_FILL_EVENT, onStatus)
      void confirmViaApi(msgId, idx, action)
    }, 60_000)

InvoiceFormPage.tsx (one-shot consume, render-time only):

  if (liveFillRef.current === undefined) {
    liveFillRef.current = isEdit ? null : consumeLiveFill('create_invoice')
  }

liveFill.ts requestLiveFill: sessionStorage.setItem(KEY, JSON.stringify(payload)) — only consumeLiveFill ever removes it.

App.tsx:79: <Route path="invoices/new" element={<InvoiceFormPage />} /> — same-path navigate() does not remount the element, so consumeLiveFill is never re-run.
confirmed.claim: The panel is a docked flex sibling, so the user can confirm a create_invoice action while /invoices/new is already open (or confirm two same-tool actions back-to-back). confirmLive writes the payload to sessionStorage and calls navigate('/invoices/new'); React Router keeps the already-mounted InvoiceFormPage, whose liveFillRef one-shot consume already ran on its first render, so consumeLiveFill is never called again and the payload is never cleared. After 60s the panel's timeout silently executes the write via POST /api/copilot/confirm (the invoice IS created), but the orphaned payload remains in sessionStorage for the rest of the tab session — surviving reloads. The next time the user opens /invoices/new for any unrelated purpose, the form consumes the stale payload and auto-runs the takeover script, which ends in an unconditional POST /api/invoices — creating a second, duplicate invoice with zero user confirmation at that moment (the banner even says 'gespeichert wird automatisch'). The same trace applies to create_cost_estimate on /cost-estimates/new. Additionally, the done/failed event listener matches only detail.tool (CopilotPanel.tsx:302 'detail.tool !== action.tool'), so when two same-tool actions are pending, the first form's 'done' event marks the second, never-executed action card 'Erledigt ✓' — a false success report on top of the latent duplicate write.

verdict.reasoning: Trace holds end-to-end: CopilotPanel is a docked sibling in AppLayout.tsx:107 (usable while /invoices/new is open); confirmLive (CopilotPanel.tsx:293-322) writes sessionStorage via requestLiveFill, navigates, and after 60s with no LIVE_FILL_EVENT falls back to confirmViaApi which POSTs /api/copilot/confirm (creates the invoice). Routes in App.tsx have no key prop, so same-path navigate keeps InvoiceFormPage mounted, and its one-shot render-time consume (InvoiceFormPage.tsx:145-149, liveFillRef === undefined guard) never re-runs — only consumeLiveFill (liveFill.ts:45-57) ever removes the key, so the payload goes stale. On the next fresh mount of /invoices/new, the consume + auto-run effect executes the takeover script ending in an unconditional POST /api/invoices (line 217) — a duplicate write with no confirmation. The detail.tool-only event match (line 302) is also as claimed. Downgrading to medium: requires the user to confirm while already on the form route (or two same-tool confirms back-to-back), and the duplicate is a same-org, user-visible invoice that can be deleted — no security/org-scoping impact.

confirmed.title: 60-second fallback timeout double-executes the confirmed write: confirmViaApi fires without cancelling the still-running form takeover script

      if (settled) return
      settled = true

      void confirmViaApi(msgId, idx, action)   // POST /api/copilot/confirm → creates invoice

InvoiceFormPage.tsx live-fill effect — start is gated on a query that can resolve arbitrarily late, and the script has no deadline or cancellation:

    ...

    await sleep(350)

confirmed.claim: Nothing cancels the form-side script when the panel's 60s timeout fires, and nothing tells the form the panel gave up. Two realistic ways the script is still alive at t=60s: (1) the run effect is gated on the 'customers-options' query — React Query retries failed fetches with exponential backoff, so on a flaky connection customerData can arrive after 60s, at which point the effect starts the full animation and POSTs /api/invoices even though confirmViaApi already created the invoice via /api/copilot/confirm; (2) the animation duration is data-dependent (14ms per description character + 350ms per position + ~3s fixed sleeps + the create round-trip), so a large position list alone can exceed 60s. Either way the same confirmed action executes twice, producing two numbered draft invoices (backend gen_invoice_number assigns a fresh number per insert; there is no idempotency key on either path). A third variant: if the form's POST times out client-side but succeeds server-side, the catch block emits status 'failed' and the panel immediately runs confirmViaApi as 'fallback' — again two invoices. Duplicate financial documents in an org where outbound invoicing reaches real customers.

verdict.reasoning: Traced end-to-end. CopilotPanel.tsx:316-321 fires confirmViaApi after 60s with no mechanism to cancel the form script — liveFill.ts has no cancel signal, only the one-shot consumeLiveFill (which the form already consumed on first render at InvoiceFormPage.tsx:148, so it doesn't help). The form effect (line 154) starts whenever customerData eventually resolves, even after 60s, and its run() then POSTs /api/invoices (line 217); meanwhile the panel's confirmViaApi hits /api/copilot/confirm whose _create_invoice (backend/app/services/copilot/tools.py:459) calls inv_routes._create — both paths insert a fresh numbered invoice, no idempotency key on either. The third variant is the most realistic trigger: if the form's POST throws client-side after the server committed, the catch (InvoiceFormPage.tsx:247-252) emits status 'failed' and the panel's onStatus branch (CopilotPanel.tsx:313) immediately runs confirmViaApi → guaranteed second invoice. Downgraded to medium: the triggering windows are narrow (customers query resolving >60s late, >~55s of animation text, or a torn POST), and the duplicates are draft invoices requiring a manual send step before reaching real customers.

confirmed.title: Live-fill customer resolution diverges from the backend confirm path: first-substring-match over a 500-row page can attach the document to the wrong customer, and a no-match silently saves a customer-less invoice the UI otherwise forbids
confirmed.claim: The same confirmed create_invoice/create_cost_estimate action resolves the customer differently depending on which execution path wins. The backend tool (_resolve_customer, used by the /api/copilot/confirm fallback) refuses ambiguous name matches and searches name+email+customer_number without a page cap; the live-fill script instead takes the FIRST case-insensitive substring hit of full_name within the first 500 customers and proceeds. With args.customer = 'Müller' and several Müllers in the org, the live fill silently attaches the invoice to whichever Müller sorts first, while the fallback path would have asked for clarification — so the record written depends on a race, not on what was confirmed. Worse, when there is no match at all (customer past the limit=500 page, or matched only by email/number), cid stays '' and the script still POSTs with customer_id: null — creating a customer-less invoice that the form's own save button (disabled={!customerId}) and the backend copilot tool ('Kein aktiver Kunde … gefunden') both refuse to produce. The user has no abort: the script is not cancelled on unmount, so even navigating away mid-animation does not stop the save, and navigate(`/invoices/${inv.id}`) then yanks them to the result.

verdict.reasoning: Traced the full path: CopilotPanel.tsx confirmLive() routes confirmed create_invoice/create_cost_estimate to live fill BEFORE the backend resolver ever runs, and orchestrator.py passes raw LLM args (tool schema says customer = "Name/Kundennummer/UUID", not even required), so names do reach the form. InvoiceFormPage.tsx does first .find() substring match on full_name only over /api/customers?limit=500 (no ambiguity check, no email/number match), vs tools.py _resolve_customer which returns ambiguous/error — genuine divergence. On no match it POSTs customer_id: null, and the backend accepts it: validate_fk_in_org skips when fk_id is None, so a customer-less draft invoice is created and the panel reports "done" (fallback to confirmViaApi only fires on thrown errors/timeout), exactly the state the manual save button (disabled={!customerId}) forbids. Medium severity is right: draft-only artifact and admin-confirmed action, but wrong-customer attachment is silent.

confirmed.title: KVA live-fill: user watches the form and PDF preview filled with the org's default intro/closing/payment texts, but the auto-save persists empty strings — what is seen diverges from what is written
confirmed.claim: During a create_cost_estimate takeover, the text-defaults effect runs independently of the live-fill script and visibly fills Einleitungstext/Schlusstext/Zahlungsbedingungen in the form; the debounced live PDF preview renders those defaults too. But when the copilot args carry no intro_text/closing_text (the common case — the LLM tool schema rarely supplies them), the script's auto-save POSTs intro_text: '', closing_text: '', and always payment_terms: '' — so the persisted KVA, the document later sent to a real customer, is missing the payment-terms and intro/closing texts the user just watched Kiki 'fill in'. A manual save of the identically-looking form would persist the defaults (the save mutation serializes the introText/closingText/paymentTerms state). This directly breaks the protocol's stated invariant that the animation cannot diverge from what is persisted, and it is silent: the user is navigated to the saved KVA only after the confirmation note 'live ausgefüllt & gespeichert'. The invoice form has the same hardcoding (payment_terms_text: '', payment_terms_days: 14, dates forced to today at InvoiceFormPage.tsx:225-233), though there the visible defaults happen to coincide, so the KVA page is where the user-visible divergence is real.

verdict.reasoning: Traced end-to-end. The text-defaults effect (CostEstimateFormPage.tsx:166-173) fills introText/closingText/paymentTerms state unconditionally for new docs, and the debounced preview payload serializes that state — so the user sees the org defaults in form and PDF preview during the takeover. The live-fill auto-save builds its own payload and hardcodes `payment_terms: ''` and empties intro/closing when copilot args omit them; the backend create (_build_row in backend/app/api/routes/cost_estimates.py) persists the payload verbatim with no server-side default fill, and the PDF builder (backend/app/services/cost_estimates.py:288, 339-347) only falls back to a generic intro string and entirely omits closing_text/payment_terms when empty — so the document sent to a real customer lacks the org's payment terms despite the user having watched them appear. Medium is right: data-quality/UX divergence on customer-facing documents, no security impact, user can still edit before sending.

confirmed.title: Preisauskunft-OFF claims success while the price doc may stay attached to the live agent (DB/EL divergence + permanent orphan)
confirmed.claim: When an org disables Preisauskunft (PATCH /kiki-zentrale/price-info, kiki_zentrale.py:1161 schedules sync_price_list_kb), the removal path swallows every exception from the EL detach/delete, then the `finally` block unconditionally clears agent_configs.price_list_doc_id, and the caller returns {"synced": True, "doc_id": None}. If patch_agent_safely raises (PATCH >=300, VerificationFailedError with rollback — rollback restores the KB array including the doc — or a transient httpx error), the price document remains attached to the live agent with usage_mode "auto" while the DB says no document exists. The doc id is now untracked, so no code path can ever detach or delete it: it survives a later re-enable (sync only deletes old_doc_id read from the now-NULL column), leaving a permanently attached stale price list whose Richtpreise the RAG layer keeps feeding the agent on real customer calls. The only signal is a logger.warning; the API response to the admin reports success.

verdict.reasoning: Verified in price_knowledge.py:139-162: the except swallows all EL errors and the finally unconditionally NULLs price_list_doc_id; patch_agent_safely (elevenlabs_agent.py:349-361) rolls back the snapshot — restoring the doc in the KB array — before raising VerificationFailedError, so the doc can stay attached while the DB says none exists. Re-enable attaches the new doc with merge_arrays=[KB_PATH] (line 121), so the orphan is never pruned; no reconciliation path exists. Downgraded to medium: requires a transient EL failure at the detach call, the orphaned content is stale-but-formerly-valid prices, and the swallow is a documented "best-effort" design — but the permanent untracked attachment serving stale Richtpreise on real calls is a genuine silent-failure bug.

(b) attach succeeds but the agent_configs update throws → the new doc Y is attached but price_list_doc_id still holds old X; the next sync reads old_doc_id=X, creates Z, attaches it, deletes X — Y stays attached and untracked forever, so the agent carries two same-named price docs and RAG can serve the stale one. The attach uses merge_arrays (additive), removal is only by the single stored price_list_doc_id (never by DOC_NAME), and the blanket except at line 134 swallows partial failures with no compensation — so a failed DB update after attach (b) or a failed _detach_and_delete with clear_column=False (c) leaves a stale price doc attached to the agent indefinitely, and (a) orphans workspace docs. confirmed.claim: Five endpoints each schedule sync_price_list_kb as a fire-and-forget BackgroundTask with no per-org lock or idempotency: two near-simultaneous catalog saves (e.g. CSV import plus an item edit, or rapid PATCHes from the UI) run two syncs concurrently for the same org. Both read the same price_list_doc_id=X, both create a fresh doc (Y and Z), both attach via merge (KB array now holds X, Y, Z — _union_list dedupes by id, so both survive), both write the column (last-writer-wins, say Z), both try to delete X — leaving Y attached to the agent but tracked nowhere, a permanent duplicate price doc. Worse, _detach_and_delete is a GET-prune-PUT replacement (merge_arrays=[] replaces the whole knowledge_base array with a snapshot taken before the patch): any document attached between its GET and PATCH — the concurrent sync's new price doc, or a knowledge_resources document pushed via push_knowledge_resource_to_elevenlabs (elevenlabs_agent.py:646) — is silently detached, and _verify's list check (`len(actual or []) < len(new)`) cannot catch it because the replaced array exactly equals `new`. remove_knowledge_resource_from_elevenlabs (elevenlabs_agent.py:720-733) has the identical stale-replacement window.

verdict.reasoning: Verified end-to-end: 5 endpoints schedule sync_price_list_kb as a sync BackgroundTask (threadpool, concurrent across requests) with no lock/idempotency; the read-create-attach-update-delete sequence makes two concurrent syncs leave one untracked doc permanently attached. _detach_and_delete writes a stale full knowledge_base snapshot with merge_arrays=[] (whole-array replace), and _verify's len(actual)<len(new) check cannot detect entries dropped by the replacement; remove_knowledge_resource_from_elevenlabs shares the pattern. One correction: the blast radius is same-org only (per-org agents + cross-org guard in patch_agent_safely), not "other orgs"; severity medium is appropriate.

confirmed.title: hk_queryKnowledgeBase stub is attached to every agent and prompted as the answer source, but always returns "no information" — contradicting the real attached Preisliste/knowledge docs
confirmed.claim: Every HeyKiki agent carries the hk_queryKnowledgeBase tool (HK_TOOL_NAMES, agent_config.py:97) and the outbound prompt explicitly instructs the agent to use it for company-specific factual questions (outbound_occasions.py:128), yet the backend stub unconditionally returns success:true with the authoritative German message "Dazu liegen mir keine Informationen vor" regardless of org or query. For orgs with Preisauskunft enabled, the system prompt (agent_config.py:410) tells the agent to quote only from the native KB document "Preisliste (Richtpreise)" — but nothing stops the LLM from calling the similarly-named tool first for a price or service question; the tool then asserts no information exists, contradicting the attached document and steering the agent to tell a real customer (outbound is LIVE) that no price/answer is available even when the synced price list contains it. The success:true framing makes this a silent failure: no log, no error, the agent treats the empty answer as a definitive lookup result.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all four links: knowledge.py stub unconditionally returns success:true with the authoritative "keine Informationen" message; the tool is in HK_TOOL_NAMES for every agent; agent_prompt_template.txt:746 explicitly lists "Preise" and "hochgeladene PDFs" as reasons to call it FIRST ("Rufe IMMER zuerst dieses Tool auf"); meanwhile price_knowledge.py syncs the real Preisliste into the ElevenLabs native knowledge_base that agent_config.py:410 tells the agent to quote from. No guard excludes price questions from the stub, so the tool can assert no info exists while the attached doc contains the answer — a genuine silent-contradiction path on live outbound calls. Medium is right: native RAG may still surface the doc, so failure is intermittent, not guaranteed.

confirmed.title: Dead forwarding_number fallback: _fetch_kz_config never selects the column, so the emergency transfer is silently absent for forwarding-only orgs
confirmed.evidence: build_transfer_tool (agent_config.py:1321): `emergency = _dial_clean(cfg.get("emergency_number") or cfg.get("forwarding_number"))` — and render_emergency_block (agent_config.py:795): `if (cfg.get("emergency_number") or cfg.get("forwarding_number") or "").strip():`. But the only cfg source, _fetch_kz_config (agent_config.py:377-387), selects: `"...emergency_enabled, emergency_number, emergency_only_outside_business_hours, emergency_keywords, emergency_extra_windows, emergency_surcharge_notice_enabled, emergency_surcharge_text, incoming_forwarding_number, price_info_enabled, ..."` — `forwarding_number` is NOT in the column list, so `cfg.get("forwarding_number")` is always None.
confirmed.claim: Both the transfer_to_number tool builder and the emergency prompt block deliberately fall back from emergency_number to forwarding_number (the Telefon-screen field whose handler docstring in kiki_zentrale.py says 'these drive the transferCall tool at runtime'). That fallback can never fire because _fetch_kz_config — the sole cfg provider for sync_system_tools_for_org and render_prompt_for_org — omits forwarding_number from its SELECT. An org with emergency_enabled=true that entered its emergency target only as forwarding_number gets NO emergency transfer entry on the agent and a prompt that says 'Es ist KEINE Notdienst-Nummer hinterlegt — leite NICHT weiter'; the save returns 200 and the sync banner shows applied. With outbound live, a confirmed emergency is silently downgraded from a live bridge to a callback note. Fix is adding forwarding_number to the _fetch_kz_config select list.

verdict.reasoning: Verified the select string in _fetch_kz_config (agent_config.py:385) omits forwarding_number while both render_emergency_block (line 794) and build_transfer_tool (line 1321) fall back to it; _fetch_kz_config is the only cfg source for both (lines 996, 1439). The column is real and legacy-populated (transfer.py:83 selects it with an explicit legacy-fallback comment; the kiki_zentrale route still reads/writes it), and no rescue path exists: the prompt's else-branch tells the agent NOT to transfer and the webhook fallback is no longer referenced by the prompt per build_transfer_tool's own docstring. Legacy-only orgs with emergency_enabled lose live emergency transfer silently.

confirmed.title: Staff-transfer dedupe ignores emergency_enabled: tool removed entirely while the prompt still instructs the agent to call transfer_to_number
confirmed.evidence: agent_config.py:1321-1347: `emergency = _dial_clean(cfg.get("emergency_number") or cfg.get("forwarding_number"))` is computed UNCONDITIONALLY; the emergency entry is gated by `if emergency and cfg.get("emergency_enabled"):`, but the staff entry is gated only by `if staff and staff != emergency:`. With emergency_enabled=False and emergency_number equal to incoming_forwarding_number, neither entry is appended → `if not transfers: return None` → sync_system_tools_for_org pushes `"transfer_to_number": None`, deleting the tool. Meanwhile render_staff_transfer_block (agent_config.py:818) gates only on `number_set = bool((cfg.get("incoming_forwarding_number") or "").strip())` and emits: 'rufe das System-Werkzeug `transfer_to_number` auf (Mitarbeiter-Weiterleitung)'.
confirmed.claim: The `staff != emergency` dedupe was written to avoid two transfer entries with the same destination, but it compares against the emergency number even when the emergency transfer was NOT added (emergency_enabled=False). A realistic config — emergency service toggled off, with the boss's mobile stored in both emergency_number and incoming_forwarding_number — produces an empty transfers list, so the transfer_to_number system tool is removed from the agent, while the simultaneously re-rendered prompt (which checks only that incoming_forwarding_number is set) still tells the agent to invoke transfer_to_number for staff handoffs. Callers asking for a human hit a missing-tool failure instead of a live transfer. The dedupe should only apply when the emergency entry was actually appended.

verdict.reasoning: Verified at agent_config.py:1321-1347 that `emergency` is computed unconditionally (with a forwarding_number fallback) and the staff entry's `staff != emergency` dedupe applies even when emergency_enabled is False and no emergency entry was appended, yielding `transfers == []` → None. Traced None through patch_agent_safely: _deep_merge replaces the tool object with None and _verify's clobber guard checks only the separate `tools` array (TOOLS_PATH), not built_in_tools, so the system tool is genuinely deleted without rollback. Meanwhile render_staff_transfer_block (line 818) gates only on incoming_forwarding_number being set and still instructs the agent to call `transfer_to_number`, producing the prompt/tool mismatch exactly as claimed.

confirmed.title: _restore_full rollback is unverified and partial: PATCH status ignored, platform_settings never restored, yet the audit and exception claim a successful rollback
confirmed.claim: Three defects in the safety net's rollback path: (1) the rollback PATCH's HTTP status is never checked — if ElevenLabs rejects or 5xx's the restore, the agent stays in the broken post-write state while agent_writes_audit records rolled_back=True and the raised error tells operators the agent was rolled back; (2) the restore body carries only conversation_config and name, but patch_agent_safely is also used for platform_settings writes (configure_agent B.4 webhook URL and B.6 overrides whitelist, with required_override_flags=True verification) — a verify failure on those writes 'rolls back' nothing of what was written; (3) per the codebase's own empirically-established ElevenLabs PATCH semantics (_build_patch_body docstring: 'ElevenLabs deep-merges nested conversation_config objects'), a restore PATCH of the old config cannot DELETE keys the failed write added (e.g. a newly-added built_in_tools entry), so rollback after a verify failure can leave partially-applied new tool config in place. All three paths produce a false 'rolled back to snapshot' audit trail on the system guarding live emergency-transfer config.

verdict.reasoning: Traced all three defects: _patch_agent (lines 103-107) returns the httpx.Response without raising, and _restore_full (447-452) discards it, so a failed restore still yields rolled_back=True audit and a 'rolled back to snapshot' error message (354-363). patch_agent_safely IS used for platform_settings writes (agent_config.py 1229 webhook, 1267 overrides whitelist), yet _restore_full only restores conversation_config and name. The codebase's own documented EL deep-merge PATCH semantics mean the restore cannot delete keys an aborted write added. No other guard or rollback path exists.

confirmed.title: No per-agent serialization: concurrent background pushes can verify-fail on each other and roll the agent back to a pre-save snapshot, reverting the other save's config
confirmed.evidence: patch_agent_safely (elevenlabs_agent.py:298-355): `current = get_agent_config(agent_id)` ... `resp = _patch_agent(agent_id, body)` ... `post = get_agent_config(agent_id)` ... `if not ok: _restore_full(agent_id, current)` — no lock or version check between the GET and the rollback. Callers run concurrently: every KZ save schedules `background.add_task(_repush_bg, ...)` (kiki_zentrale.py:107-113), and _repush_bg performs TWO sequential patch_agent_safely calls for emergency/phone saves (prompt push + `ac.sync_transfer_tool_for_org(org_id)`). The seq guard explicitly protects only the DB banner row (agent_config.py:1474-1478: 'finish_sync only writes when its seq is still the latest ... a slow old push can never overwrite a newer pending state') — there is no equivalent guard on the ElevenLabs writes themselves.
confirmed.claim: Two rapid saves for the same org spawn two concurrent _repush_bg threads (FastAPI background tasks for sync callables run in the threadpool), each doing GET → PATCH → re-GET verify against the same agent with no mutual exclusion. Interleaving A-PATCH, B-PATCH, A-verify makes A's verify read B's value for the contested leaf ('{path} did not apply') and triggers `_restore_full(agent_id, current_A)`, where current_A is a snapshot taken before BOTH writes — B's just-applied transfer-tool or prompt change is silently reverted on the ElevenLabs side even though B's own verify passed and the DB sync banner reports B as applied. The agent_sync_seq mechanism was built for exactly this overlap but only fences the status row, not the agent config; the result is a stale-rollback race on live emergency-transfer configuration.

verdict.reasoning: Verified end-to-end: patch_agent_safely (backend/app/services/elevenlabs_agent.py:298-363) does GET → PATCH → re-GET verify → on failure `_restore_full(agent_id, current)`, and `_restore_full` (line 447-452) PATCHes the ENTIRE conversation_config from the pre-write snapshot — so a rollback wipes any concurrent writer's change, not just the contested leaf. There is no lock anywhere (grep for Lock/threading in elevenlabs_agent.py and agent_config.py finds nothing), and the seq guard in finish_sync (agent_config.py:1507-1525) only fences the DB sync-banner row, never the EL write. Callers are genuinely concurrent: every save calls background.add_task(_repush_bg, ...) (kiki_zentrale.py:107-113), and sync background tasks run in Starlette's threadpool, so two rapid saves run two interleavable GET/PATCH/verify sequences against the same agent; _verify uses exact equality on the prompt leaf (line 401: `actual != new` → "did not apply"), so if B's differently-rendered prompt lands between A's PATCH and A's re-GET, A rolls the whole agent back to current_A (pre-both-writes) while B's banner shows applied. It requires an unlucky interleaving AND that A rendered from DB before B's synchronous DB write, so probability is low — medium severity (live emergency-transfer config silently reverted) is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Transfer destination numbers are persisted with zero validation; _dial_clean cannot reject, so a typo yields an undialable emergency target or silently drops the transfer entry
confirmed.evidence: EmergencyUpdate (kiki_zentrale.py:224-232): `emergency_number: str | None = None` — no validator; update_emergency writes `payload.model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` straight to agent_configs. PhoneUpdate (kiki_zentrale.py:253-290) HAS a `_looks_like_phone` E.164 check but applies it ONLY via `cleaned_existing_business_number()`; `forwarding_number` and `incoming_forwarding_number` are written raw (`payload.model_dump(exclude_unset=True, exclude={"existing_business_number"})`). At sync time _dial_clean (agent_config.py:1297-1308) only normalizes: `n = re.sub(r"[^\d+]", "", number or "")` then prefixes '+'/'+49' — it never rejects by length or shape; a fully non-numeric entry returns "" and the transfer entry is silently omitted (`if emergency and ...`).
confirmed.claim: The two fields that become live conference-transfer destinations on the ElevenLabs agent (emergency_number, incoming_forwarding_number) bypass the phone validator that the very same Pydantic model defines for the cosmetic existing_business_number field. A malformed entry (digits-stripped garbage, a 3-digit fragment like '12' → '+12', an over-long paste) either silently removes the emergency transfer entry or installs an undialable E.164-lookalike on the agent; in both cases the save returns 200 and the sync banner resolves 'applied' (the EL write of the malformed string succeeds — _verify only checks the value landed). The failure surfaces only at the moment a confirmed emergency caller is being bridged, as a dead transfer. Applying the existing _looks_like_phone check (or making _dial_clean reject implausible results) to emergency_number/forwarding_number/incoming_forwarding_number closes it.

verdict.reasoning: Verified end-to-end. Backend: update_emergency (kiki_zentrale.py:1222-1233) writes payload.model_dump(exclude_unset=True) straight to agent_configs with no validation on emergency_number; update_phone (1238-1290) calls cleaned_existing_business_number() only for the cosmetic organizations field while forwarding_number/incoming_forwarding_number go raw into agent_configs (exclude={"existing_business_number"}). Frontend (ConfigSections.tsx:711) is a free-text input with no pattern/validation, so a UI typo reaches the DB. _dial_clean (agent_config.py:1297-1308) only strips/prefixes and never rejects — garbage like "abc" yields "" causing the emergency transfer entry to be silently omitted (`if emergency and cfg.get("emergency_enabled")`), and a fragment like "12" becomes the undialable "+12". No DB constraint or downstream guard rejects it; failure surfaces only during a live emergency bridge. Medium severity is appropriate (admin self-misconfiguration, but silent failure on the emergency path).

confirmed.title: voicemail_detection references {{voicemailMessage}} that only outbound calls supply; inbound conversation-init sends no value and no placeholder default exists
confirmed.evidence: build_voicemail_tool (agent_config.py:1385-1388): `"params": {"system_tool_type": "voicemail_detection", "voicemail_message": "{{voicemailMessage}}"}` — pushed unconditionally onto the org's main agent by sync_system_tools_for_org. The inbound conversation-initiation webhook's _empty_vars/customer vars (conversation_init.py:23-31, 95-103) contain only customer_* keys — no voicemailMessage — and nothing in the codebase sets an agent-level dynamic_variable_placeholders default.
confirmed.claim: The voicemail_detection system tool on the shared inbound/outbound agent embeds the dynamic variable {{voicemailMessage}}, but only the outbound dispatch path provides a value; inbound calls (the agent's primary mode) leave it undefined with no placeholder default configured on the agent. If the tool ever fires on an inbound leg — or if ElevenLabs validates referenced dynamic variables at conversation start — voicemail_message resolves to nothing or the literal placeholder text rather than a real message. Severity is low because the tool's hardened description steers it away from inbound triggers, but the contract gap (variable referenced in always-on agent config, supplied only by one call direction) is real and cheap to close with a placeholder default.

verdict.reasoning: Verified all three legs: agent_config.py:1387 hardcodes {{voicemailMessage}} into voicemail_detection and sync_system_tools_for_org pushes it unconditionally to the org's main (inbound-serving) agent; conversation_init.py supplies only customer_* dynamic variables with no voicemailMessage; the only supplier is outbound_occasions.py:865. Grep confirms no dynamic_variable_placeholders default exists anywhere in backend/app. The contract mismatch is real but impact is bounded by the hardened tool description steering away from inbound triggers, so low severity stands.
